title: Casting Evaluation: Methods and Discontinuities
narrationScript: Castings punish radiographers who do not respect them. A weld is a controlled, layered process laid down by a person you could interview afterward. A casting is a single violent event. Liquid metal at well over two thousand degrees Fahrenheit pours into a mold, flows, cools, and shrinks, and every discontinuity you will ever find on the film was decided in those few minutes, inside a closed mold nobody could see. So before we read casting film, we learn the process. Start with the methods. Sand casting is the workhorse. A pattern is packed in sand inside a two part flask. The top half is the cope, the bottom half is the drag, and they meet at the parting line. Molten metal pours down a sprue, travels along a runner, and enters the cavity through a gate. Risers are reservoirs of extra liquid metal that feed the casting as it shrinks. Sand molds are permeable, a little rough, and they can wash, so sand castings carry the broadest mix of discontinuities. Gas, shrink, sand inclusions, all of it. Investment casting builds a ceramic shell around a wax pattern, melts the wax out, and pours into the fired shell. It holds fine detail and tight tolerances, which is why aerospace leans on it, but thin sections and long flow paths make it prone to misruns, cold shuts, and fine shrinkage. Permanent mold casting pours by gravity into a reusable metal mold that chills the metal quickly and refines the grain. Die casting injects metal into a steel die under high pressure. The turbulence traps gas, so die castings are famous for fine dispersed porosity, and that trapped gas is why most die castings cannot be weld repaired or heat treated without blistering. Centrifugal casting spins the mold while the metal solidifies from the outer wall inward. Spinning throws dense, clean metal outward, and the lighter junk, gas, dross, and inclusions, collects at the inner bore, which is usually machined away. Now the physics that drives almost everything you will see, shrinkage. Metal shrinks three times. It shrinks as a cooling liquid. It shrinks as it transforms from liquid to solid, and that step is the killer, several percent of volume for most alloys. Then it shrinks again as a cooling solid. The riser exists to feed the liquid to solid stage. As long as a channel of liquid metal connects the riser to every region that is still solidifying, the casting feeds itself and stays sound. When a region freezes off and gets cut from the feed path, the missing volume has to come from somewhere, so it appears as a void. That is shrinkage. It concentrates at thermal centers, the thick sections, bosses, and junctions where ribs meet walls, the places shaped like the letters L, T, V and X in cross section, because those regions hold heat and freeze last. Remember the rule. Follow the last metal to freeze. Shrinkage shows up in recognizable forms. A gross cavity. A spongy, interconnected network. Fine filamentary threads that follow the structure of the growing crystals. And scattered micro shrinkage between grains. Gas porosity is a different animal, rounded voids from dissolved gas coming out of solution, from mold moisture, or from poor venting. Gas bubbles are buoyant, so in a conventionally poured casting they drift upward toward the cope surface. Hot tears happen while the metal is still partly mushy and has almost no strength. The casting tries to contract, the mold or a heavy neighboring section refuses to let it, and the metal tears. Hot tears are ragged and branched, and they live at section changes and other points of restraint. A crack, by contrast, forms after the metal is fully solid and runs finer and straighter. Cold shuts form when two streams of metal meet too cold to fuse, leaving a smooth seam, usually far from the gate where the metal arrives last and coldest. A misrun is the same disease at a worse stage. The metal froze before the cavity even filled. Then come the foreign bodies. Sand washed off the mold, slag and dross carried in with the pour, and the hardware, the chaplets and internal chills that were supposed to fuse into the casting and sometimes do not. Core shift leaves one wall thin and the opposite wall thick. Finally, think about orientation, because it decides what your beam can see. Gas and shrinkage are volumetric, and they show from almost any angle. Hot tears, cracks, and cold shuts are planar. If the beam crosses a tight planar tear broadside, the film may show nothing at all. Knowing where each discontinuity forms and how it lies is what lets you pick shot angles that find it. That is the whole point of this lesson.
imagePrompt: A foundry worker in a silver heat suit pours glowing molten steel from a ladle into a sand mold flask while a radiographer in the foreground chalks shot locations on a cooled rough casting.

sections.heading: Why Casting Knowledge Comes First
sections.body:
A weld is built layer by layer under a welder's control, and most weld discontinuities trace back to a moment the interpreter can reconstruct. A casting is different: every discontinuity was decided in the few minutes between pour and solidification, inside a closed mold nobody could watch. That is why the CP-105 interpretation outline puts casting method review ahead of casting film reading — the image only makes sense when you can replay the event that produced it.

Process knowledge does three jobs for a Level II:

- It predicts **what** is plausible. Die castings rarely contain sand inclusions; sand castings rarely show the fine, dispersed porosity typical of die work.
- It predicts **where** to look. Shrinkage hunts thermal centers; gas drifts toward the cope; cold shuts hide far from the gates.
- It predicts **orientation**, which decides whether a given beam angle can even see the discontinuity.

Without that knowledge, interpretation is pattern-matching. With it, evaluation becomes prediction — confirmed or corrected by the film.

sections.heading: Casting Method Review
sections.body:
**Sand casting** packs molding sand around a pattern inside a two-part flask — the **cope** on top, the **drag** below, meeting at the **parting line**. Metal pours through a **sprue**, runs along a **runner**, and enters the cavity through a **gate**; **risers** store extra liquid metal to feed shrinkage, and **cores** form internal passages. Sand is permeable and slightly rough, and loose sand can wash into the cavity, so sand castings show the widest discontinuity mix: gas, shrinkage, inclusions, hot tears.

**Investment casting** dips a wax pattern in ceramic slurry, fires the shell, melts out the wax, and pours. Excellent detail and surface finish — the aerospace favorite — but thin sections and long flow paths invite misruns, cold shuts, and fine shrinkage.

**Permanent mold casting** pours by gravity into a reusable metal mold; faster chilling refines grain and improves soundness near the mold wall.

**Die casting** injects metal under high pressure. Turbulent filling traps gas as fine dispersed porosity — and that gas is why die castings generally cannot be weld-repaired or solution heat treated without blistering.

**Centrifugal casting** spins the mold so dense metal is thrown outward while gas, dross, and light inclusions concentrate at the inner bore, normally removed by machining. **Continuous casting** solidifies long uniform shapes through a water-cooled die, with centerline shrinkage the classic risk.

sections.caption: Cross-section of a sand mold showing the gating system and the typical birthplace of each major casting discontinuity.

sections.heading: Solidification and Feeding: The Physics of Shrink
sections.body:
Metal shrinks three times on its way to a casting. It contracts as a cooling **liquid**, contracts again — several percent of volume for most alloys — during the **liquid-to-solid transformation**, and contracts once more as a cooling **solid**. The middle stage is the dangerous one, and the riser exists to feed it.

A casting stays sound as long as liquid feed metal can reach every region that is still solidifying. Founders encourage **directional (progressive) solidification** — freezing that starts at thin extremities and sweeps back toward the riser — using riser placement and **chills** (metal inserts that pull heat out of selected spots). When a region freezes off early and the feed path closes, the volume deficit appears as a void: **shrinkage**.

Shrinkage concentrates at **thermal centers** — thick sections, bosses, and especially the junctions where ribs and walls meet. Sections shaped like the letters **L, T, V, X, and Y** in cross-section hold heat at the junction and freeze last. Depending on when feeding failed, shrinkage takes the form of a gross **cavity**, an interconnected **sponge**, fine branching **filamentary or dendritic** threads, or scattered intergranular **micro-shrinkage**.

sections.caption: Casting methods and the discontinuities each one favors.
rows: Gas porosity, shrinkage, sand inclusions, hot tears, core shift
Ceramic shell, fine detail, thin sections
Misruns, cold shuts, fine shrinkage, shell inclusions
Cold shuts and misruns from early freezing; junction shrink
Fine dispersed gas porosity; cold fills; no weld repair
Gas, dross, and inclusions banded at the inner bore

sections.heading: The Discontinuity Catalog
sections.body:
- **Gas porosity / blowholes** — rounded voids from dissolved gas, mold moisture, or poor venting; larger smooth holes are blowholes. Buoyant, so they drift toward the cope surface and collect under cores.
- **Shrinkage** — cavity, sponge, filamentary, or micro form, at thermal centers cut off from feed.
- **Hot tears** — ragged, branched separations formed while the metal was still partly mushy and weak, where the mold or a heavy section restrained contraction. Always suspect section changes.
- **Cracks (cold cracks)** — finer, straighter separations formed after full solidification, including stress cracks from rough handling or quenching.
- **Cold shuts** — smooth, linear seams where two metal streams met too cold to fuse; typically far from the gate. A **misrun** is the terminal case: the cavity never filled.
- **Inclusions** — washed-in **sand**, **slag**, or **dross** (oxide skins, common in aluminum); also **high-density inclusions** from contaminating metals.
- **Unfused chaplets and chills** — core-support hardware and internal chills that failed to fuse into the surrounding metal.
- **Core shift / mold shift** — mismatch that thins one wall and thickens the opposite one.
- **Segregation** — local variation in alloy chemistry; subtle on film but real in service.

sections.caption: Four rules of thumb for predicting where casting discontinuities form and how they lie.

sections.heading: Origin and Orientation: Predicting the Find
sections.body:
Every casting discontinuity has an address and an attitude, and both come from the process.

- **Shrinkage** lives at thermal centers — junctions, bosses, sections under an undersized riser. It is volumetric, so beam angle is forgiving, though filamentary shrink roughly follows the dendritic growth direction.
- **Hot tears** form at points of restraint — section changes, corners, regions the mold gripped — and lie roughly perpendicular to the direction the casting was trying to contract. They are **planar**: a tight tear seen broadside by the beam can vanish from the film.
- **Cracks** follow handling and residual stress; planar and fine, they demand favorable beam alignment.
- **Gas porosity** rises — expect it toward the cope surface and trapped under cores.
- **Sand and slag inclusions** ride the metal stream: look near gates and along flow paths; dross floats toward upper surfaces.
- **Cold shuts** sit at flow confluences far from gates, where two cold fronts met; smooth, planar, and angle-sensitive.
- **Unfused chaplets** are wherever the core prints say the hardware was placed.

This is why critical castings get multiple, deliberately angled exposures of known junctions — the technique is aimed at where the process says trouble lives.

sections.title: Follow the last metal to freeze
sections.body: Shrinkage appears wherever solidifying metal lost its liquid feed path — thermal centers at **thick sections, bosses, and L, T, V, X junctions**. Before you ever hang film, ask where the casting froze last; that is where to expect shrink, and the section-change boundaries beside it are where to expect hot tears.

figures.title: Sand casting cross-section: where discontinuities start

checkQuestions.question: A steel casting shows a discontinuity concentrated exactly at the junction where a rib meets the main wall — the thermal center of a T-shaped section. Which discontinuity is most likely?
options: Cold shut
Shrinkage
Sand inclusion
Unfused chaplet
checkQuestions.explanation: Junctions hold heat and freeze last; when the feed path from the riser closes before they solidify, the volume deficit appears as shrinkage at the thermal center.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly separates a hot tear from a cold crack?
options: A hot tear forms after full solidification and runs straight; a crack forms in the mushy state
A hot tear forms while the metal is partly solid and weak, so it is ragged and branched at points of restraint; a cold crack forms after solidification and runs finer and straighter
Hot tears occur only in aluminum; cracks occur only in steel
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable on a radiograph report
checkQuestions.explanation: Hot tears occur during solidification when restrained contraction tears the weak mushy metal, giving ragged branched separations at section changes; cold cracks form in fully solid metal and are finer and straighter.

checkQuestions.question: A smooth, linear seam is found in a thin section of an investment casting, far from the gates. The metal on each side is sound. This origin best matches:
options: A misrun — the cavity never filled
A hot tear from mold restraint
A cold shut — two metal streams met too cold to fuse
Filamentary shrinkage following dendrite growth
checkQuestions.explanation: Cold shuts form where converging streams arrive too cold to fuse, leaving a smooth planar seam — typically in thin sections far from the gate where metal arrives last and coldest.

checkQuestions.question: In a conventionally poured (static) sand casting, gas porosity tends to concentrate:
options: Toward the cope (upper) surface and trapped under cores, because gas bubbles are buoyant
At the drag (lower) surface, because gas is denser than steel
Only inside the riser, which is discarded
Uniformly through the section regardless of position
checkQuestions.explanation: Gas bubbles rise through liquid metal, so porosity collects toward the cope surface and beneath cores or other features that block its escape.

checkQuestions.question: Why can a tight hot tear escape detection even on a film with correct density and sensitivity?
options: Hot tears are always too small for radiography to record
Hot tears are planar — if the beam crosses the plane of the tear broadside instead of along it, almost no thickness difference is presented
Hot tears absorb radiation the same as sound metal in every direction
Film cannot record discontinuities located at section changes
checkQuestions.explanation: Planar discontinuities present meaningful thickness difference only when the beam runs roughly along the plane of separation; broadside, the tight tear changes path length almost not at all, which is why critical junctions get multiple angled exposures.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course, IE 3.1–3.3
ASTM E1316 — standard terminology for nondestructive examinations (casting discontinuity definitions, general reference)
Foundry solidification and feeding literature on risering, directional solidification, and hot tearing (general reference)
